result:
What comes to me first is a window.

Not any particular window, but the idea of one: a square of permission cut into a wall. Outside it, something is always happening. Rain rehearsing its old argument with the roof. A delivery truck sighing at the curb. Leaves turning themselves over like small green animals in their sleep. A person crossing the street with one hand holding coffee and the other hand holding a life no one else can see.

Inside the window, there is a room. Maybe there is a wooden table with scratches in it, the kind that prove people have leaned hard on it over the years. Maybe there is a lamp with a warm shade. Maybe a book lies open face-down, making a tent of itself, keeping its place in the world. The room does not have to be beautiful. It only has to be entered.

I think often about how much of life is simply entry. Entering a day. Entering a conversation. Entering grief. Entering love, though we pretend love is something that enters us. There are thresholds everywhere, but most are invisible until after we have crossed them. We say, “That was the last time,” only later. The last time you were carried by a parent. The last time you played a game with absolute seriousness. The last time you believed summer was endless. The last time you saw someone and did not know you would miss them.

Memory is strange because it edits without permission. It keeps the smell of a hallway but loses the name of the person who stood there. It preserves a shirt, a laugh, the blue bowl in your grandmother’s kitchen, but not the exact words spoken. It is less like an archive than a house where the lights flicker on unpredictably. You walk past a closed door for twenty years, and then one Tuesday, because someone is peeling an orange on the train, the door opens. There you are again, smaller, barefoot, alive in a way you had forgotten.

What also comes to me is tenderness—how much of it is hidden under ordinary things. The grocery list is tenderness. The text that says “made it home” is tenderness. The hand on the small of the back while passing through a crowded place. The extra blanket folded at the end of the bed. Even frustration can be tenderness wearing armor: “Call me when you get there,” “You need to eat,” “Don’t forget your coat.” We are clumsy animals, but again and again we attempt care.

And fear comes too, of course. It always does. Fear has a key. It lets itself in and sits down before being invited. But I suspect fear is not the opposite of courage; it is the raw material from which courage is made. Courage is fear given a direction. It is the trembling phone call. The appointment made. The apology offered. The suitcase zipped. The blank page opened. Nobody becomes brave in general. We become brave in particulars, one small impossible act at a time.

There is something holy, or close to holy, in attention. To look at a thing steadily is to rescue it from the blur. A spoon in morning light. The pulse in a wrist. A dog’s ears lifting at a familiar footstep. Steam rising from soup. Someone’s face changing when they realize they are understood. The world is not lacking in miracles; it is lacking in witnesses. Or maybe it has witnesses everywhere, and each of us is one for a little while.

If I could place one thought in a jar and leave it on your table, it would be this: your life is not only its major events. It is not only the weddings, funerals, promotions, departures, diagnoses, reconciliations. It is also the ten thousand unnoticed survivals. The mornings you got up without applause. The dishes washed while your mind was elsewhere. The jokes you made because someone needed lightness. The times you softened when you could have sharpened. The days you kept going with no grand reason except that continuing was the next available form of hope.

Hope is often misunderstood. It is not certainty that things will improve. It is not denial wearing perfume. Hope is a practice, sometimes a stubborn one. It is sweeping the floor even though dust will return. Planting bulbs in October. Learning someone’s name. Saving leftovers. Voting. Teaching. Repairing. Beginning again after the evidence has made beginning again seem foolish. Hope says: I do not know what will happen, and I am still here.

Somewhere now, someone is laughing so hard they cannot finish the story. Somewhere, a child is asking a question that will inconvenience an adult into wonder. Somewhere, a nurse is adjusting a pillow. Somewhere, bread is cooling on a counter. Somewhere, two people are deciding to forgive each other without yet knowing how. Somewhere, a song is finding the exact person who needs it.

And somewhere, perhaps, there is that window again. The rain has stopped. The room is quiet. The book is still open. The table waits with all its scratches. Outside, the world continues its enormous, indifferent generosity: clouds moving, cars passing, birds making brief signatures across the sky.

Inside, there is time enough to begin.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
